summary.intent: Test the --evaluator-url mechanism for running evaluators end-to-end by building an HTTP evaluator server, wiring --evaluator-url into the live_integration.py orchestrator, and validating through a live RED-GREEN-OBSERVER cycle.
summary.outcome: Created evaluator_http_server.py, added --evaluator-url to live_integration.py, fixed an invalid model name (gpt-5.1-mini → gpt-5.1) across 6 files, and confirmed all three evaluator paths (HTTP 0.8731, Anthropic 0.88, OpenAI 0.90) return consistent scores. Also authored EXAMPLES.md with four worked evaluator-type examples and scanned the entire/checkpoints/v1 branch for secrets.

repo: live_integration.py supported --evaluator-command but not --evaluator-url; the flag had to be added to both the GREEN (optimize) and RED (score) phases independently
gpt-5.1-mini does not exist in litellm; valid alternatives are gpt-5.1 (full model, supports temperature), gpt-5.1-codex-mini (no temperature support), and gpt-5-mini (5.0 series mini)
gpt-5.1-codex-mini rejects the temperature=0.0 parameter that llm_judge.py passes by default, causing it to return score 0.0 with an error rather than a valid response
HTTP evaluator transport is transparent: --evaluator-url and --evaluator-command produce identical scores (both 0.8731) when wrapping the same evaluator script
The generate-evaluator SKILL.md artifact is near-ceiling (~0.8731) so the GREEN optimizer cannot beat the seed — known plateau behavior from previous dogfood sessions
The entire/checkpoints/v1 branch has 64 commits and 313 files (mostly .jsonl transcripts); trufflehog found 0 verified secrets and 30 false positives (conversation UUIDs matching npm token regex)

code.finding: Content-Length header must use len(encoded_bytes) not len(str) to be correct for non-ASCII content in Python 3
server_close() must be in a finally block to prevent socket leaks on unexpected exceptions
The subprocess output parsing path for LLM judges loses JSON recovery (code-fence stripping) that the optimize/score CLI handles directly — a known parity gap
Help text example referenced gpt-5.1-mini (invalid model); corrected to gpt-5.1
7 occurrences of gpt-5.1-mini used in LLM judge mocks; all updated to gpt-5.1

workflow: trufflehog (brew install trufflehog) is effective for scanning git branches; run with --only-verified first, then without for full coverage
trufflehog false-positives UUIDs in JSON as NpmTokens — always inspect flagged values in context before treating as real findings
A spec-review subagent followed by a separate code-quality-review subagent catches implementation bugs (e.g., Content-Length byte/str mismatch) that the implementer misses
ThreadingHTTPServer should be preferred over HTTPServer to handle concurrent requests during optimization runs

friction: gpt-5.1-mini was used as the example OpenAI model throughout the codebase but does not exist, causing the OpenAI judge to return invalid JSON and a null score during RED phase validation
The apparent fix (gpt-5.1-codex-mini) also failed because the codex variant rejects the temperature parameter, requiring a second round of investigation and correction
trufflehog produced 30 noisy false positives (all conversation UUIDs) that required manual grep verification before concluding the branch was clean
open_items: No pytest tests cover the actual evaluator_http_server.py behavior — only mocked httpx tests exist for the HTTP evaluator path in the CLI
live_integration.py's LLM judge subprocess output parsing is not tolerant to JSON wrapped in markdown code fences, unlike the direct optimize/score CLI path
Add a model-id validator that catches invalid LLM judge model names (like the gpt-5.1-mini mistake) at configuration time rather than at runtime
EXAMPLES.md contains documentation-only code; consider adding runnable example scripts alongside each worked example
